turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: We will hear argument next in Case No. 14짯449. On the severance question, Mr. McAllister. MR. McALLISTER: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: The joint sentencing proceeding in these cases did not violate the Eighth Amendment because each defendant received the individualized sentencing determination to which he was entitled. Each presented all of the mitigating evidence he chose to produce. The jury instructions explicitly told the jury to consider each defendant individually. In fact, there were several separate instructions, some that related only to one defendant or only to another defendant. Each 짯짯 the jury for each defendant then had to complete a specific verdict form, and this jury had already proven its ability to distinguish between these two defendants when it convicted one of some counts in the original guilt phase, of which it acquitted the other. The only way the Kansas Supreme Court gets to an Eighth Amendment error in this case 짯짯 well, really, there are two points. One, it relied upon possible, and one instance not even, violations or errors of State law to find a prejudice that 짯짯 that rose to an Eighth Amendment level. And two, it disregarded altogether, with really no explanation, the long짯standing foundational principle that juries are presumed to follow their instructions. The two State law errors, arguably, that 짯짯 the Kansas Supreme Court found, one was the admission of evidence of the sister who made the comment about Reginald may have told me he shot those people. Well, that's not even an error of State law. They said basically that's prejudicial. It might not have come in if they had been tried separately, but in fact, that evidence would be relevant to aggravator number 1, that the defendant killed multiple people. That would have been admissible evidence and that 짯짯 that just can't be an Eighth Amendment violation.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: But the Kansas 짯짯 the Kansas Court found independent of the severance question that there was constitutional error in the admission of hearsay. MR. McALLISTER: That's the third question presented in the cert position on which the Court has not taken any action yet, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG

McALLISTER: Well, and we are certainly hopeful that the Court will take another look at that second question depending on its resolution of the two questions that are in front of it today.

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY

But even that, arguably, would be relevant to the mercy consideration to show his character, the nature of this person that is being sentenced. But that's 짯짯 that was really the extent of that evidence. And that might have come in as a rebuttal to a mercy argument. We are not applying the rule 짯짯 or are we or should we apply the Rule 14 standard? MR. McALLISTER: No, not 짯짯 not for Eighth Amendment purposes, Your Honor. Kansas would propose 짯짯 and I suspect the United States can propose something as well, but it's a very high standard as an Eighth Amendment matter. And we would say 짯짯 we would be content to accept something like it's a serious risk that a defendant will not be able to receive individualized sentencing. It is sort of molding the traditional notions of joinder with the Eighth Amendment consideration, because that seems to be the only Eighth Amendment consideration here, is the individualized sentencing.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: Is it the Eighth Amendment or is it due process? MR. McALLISTER: Well, you could use a due process standard, Justice Scalia, which we would also be fine with. The question would really be, did joinder so infect the process with unfairness that the proceedings are fundamentally unfair.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: That seems like a 짯짯 a language that is relevant rather than the cruel and unusual punishments language. MR. McALLISTER: Well, and that's exactly what this Court said in Romano v. Oklahoma, a case in which the defendant's other death sentence in a separate case was admitted against the defendant in the sentencing proceeding. That sentence later got reversed, and the Oklahoma court said the jury should not have heard about his other sentence as a matter of State law. That defendant Romano said it is an Eighth Amendment violation, and it's a due process violation. The Court said the Eighth Amendment doesn't have anything to say about what's admissible really. That is State evidentiary rules, that's not an Eighth Amendment issue. And the Court went on to address the due process claim in Romano, applying that very standard. And as we suggest the Court should do here, one of the things it pointed to specifically was jury instructions that said these are the aggravating circumstances you may consider, you may not consider any others. The court said we presume the jury follows its instructions, and nothing in the instructions gave the jury here any way to give effect to that improperly admitted evidence. And that's true here to the extent Reginald Carr argues some of this evidence was really used as a nonstatutory aggravating circumstance. By statute, Kansas does not allow anything other than statutory aggravating circumstances. In the instructions 짯짯 No. 5 I think for Reginald, No. 7 for Jonathan 짯짯 very explicitly and clearly say, here are the four aggravating circumstances the State has alleged. And then the next paragraph, you may not consider anything else as an aggravating circumstance. So in our view, the most the Kansas Supreme Court could come up with is potential violation of State law if, in fact, the evidence which is minimal in the greater scheme of this proceeding that Reginald was a corrupting influence on Jonathan, if that would not have been admitted, that's really all the Kansas Supreme Court is left with to hang its hat on in finding 짯짯 in saying there is an Eighth Amendment violation here.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Ms. Kovner. SUPPORTING PETITIONER

turns.name: MS. KOVNER
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: The Kansas Supreme Court erred when it found that the State violated the Eighth Amendment by conducting joint sentencing proceedings for the crimes the Respondents committed together. Joint proceedings can enhance accuracy and fairness, as a long line of this Court's cases hold. They provide a fuller evidentiary record to juries that are assessing relative culpability, and they prevent arbitrary disparities that may arise when two juries reach inconsistent conclusions about the common facts of a single crime.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Ms. Kovner, is there any difference between the Federal government's position and the position of the State of Kansas on this question?

turns.name: MS. KOVNER
turns.text: As to the standard, I think there's a small difference. I think our proposal is that the constitutional standard is whether evidence or argument resulted in a denial of due process or the deprivation of an individualized sentencing proceeding. And that's slightly different from Kansas's standard, because Kansas is proposing a rule that involves analysis of risk, and we think that is part of the statutory rule under Rule 14, as Justice Sotomayor alluded to. It's also part of the statutory standard that states "by and large are applying," but we think the constitutional question is just, was this person deprived of a fair trial by the evidence that came in? And that's the constitutional question.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: When you said it the first time, you had "deprived of a fair trial" and then something about individualized sentencing; is that right? Is that supposed to indicate that this is deriving both from the Due Process Clause and from the Eighth Amendment?

turns.name: MS. KOVNER
turns.text: Well, the Eighth Amendment does speak to this need for an individualized sentencing determination. We would think that the Due Process Clause also requires that proceedings be individualized. But we would agree that if there were a situation where there was a joint trial and because of the evidence introduced by one, for instance, a jury simply could not give individualized consideration to a second 짯짯 second defendant, that would be a constitutional problem. And to give an example of that 짯짯 I mean, I think the example the Court suggests in Zafiro of that is where there is aggravating evidence that pertains only to one defendant, but that that aggravating evidence is just so prejudicial and so overpowering that the jury couldn't really separate the two and would be inclined to sort of judge the second one by association. So I think that's the example the Court gives in Zafiro. But it's not 짯짯

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: Don't you think individualized consideration is required even in the non짯capital cases?

turns.name: MS. KOVNER
turns.text: Absolutely, Your Honor. So I think 짯짯

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: It's really a due process consideration, isn't it?

turns.name: MS. KOVNER
turns.text: I think due process encompasses that requirement of individualized consideration. Just to address several of the points that came up during Petitioner's argument, I think 짯짯 as to the question that Justice Kagan asked about whether, when defendants are simply pointing the finger at each other, that would necessitate severance. I think that's the question that the Court addressed in Zafiro, where that was essentially the claim the defendants were making, that we're both going to point the finger at each other at trial. And the Court said that doesn't necessitate severance; that that may, in fact, increase the jury's ability to reach an accurate verdict, to reach an accurate assessment of relative culpability, and enhance fairness. So we don't think that that's a situation that would require severance.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS

turns.text: I don't think that that's prejudicial evidence, certainly not any constitutional problem with the admission of that evidence. I don't think that even Reginald Carr is asserting that that evidence couldn't have come in against him. Rather he is asserting or the State below suggested 짯짯 the State court below suggested maybe that 짯짯 maybe the State didn't know about that evidence, and maybe just as a factual matter it wouldn't have come in. But there's no constitutional unfairness with evidence coming in on a co짯defendant's case that's harmful but accurate as to you. That's something the Court said in 짯짯 in Zafiro. If it's accurate and relevant evidence that comes in on a co짯defendant's case, there's no constitutional unfairness as to you. I think Reginald's primary claim before this Court is that there was a violation of State evidentiary law because some of the other evidence, the evidence of bad influence, might not have come in under the State rules. I think this Court's cases are clear that a violation of State evidentiary law is simply a matter of State law. The State could decide that necessitates reversal, but it's not a constitutional error. That's what this Court said in Romano.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: It would depend on what is was, wouldn't it? I mean, so I'm still asking the same question. If the 짯짯 I take it that Reginald could not have introduced evidence refuting these brothers' state of mind that he was the monster. That's what you're referring to by the bad influence. So the jury might have taken that evidence into account in trying to decide whether Reginald, for other reasons than in the statute, was a sympathetic enough character not to provide the death penalty. That's conceivable. But severance is very, very rare, and joint trials are very common. And very common is the claim on an appeal in any kind of a case that it shouldn't 짯짯 it should have been severed. So do you have cases that you can think of that would be good precedent for you where a court, including particularly this Court, said, of course there might be a little prejudice here, some, but it's not enough to warrant severance? Because I think what I have to do is read the record on this point, and then I need something to compare it with.

turns.name: MS. KOVNER

turns.text: 짯짯 because I am not sure I agree with the premise. But as to whether any prejudice is a problem, I think the best case for us is Zafiro. Zafiro talks about the idea that what you need 짯짯 that joinder is presumably going to enhance fairness and accuracy. And what you need is substantial prejudice, even to have a statutory problem, and then 짯짯 even then we are going to presume that a limiting instruction would be sufficient to cure it. So that would be our best case on that point. But, Your Honor, I think the inquiry under the Eighth Amendment has to be would the Constitution have prohibited this evidence from coming in as to Reginald Carr. And we think the answer is no. Any evidence that's relevant to an individual defendant 짯짯

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: I think I missed something you said, but 짯짯 forgive me. But the constitutional standard that you're proposing, is that a constitutional standard for both the guilt phase and the sentencing phase? And is it also for both non짯capital and capital? turns.name: MS. KOVNER
turns.text: I think that's right, Your Honor. I mean, that 짯짯 because we think that the due process standard essentially subsumes the requirement of individualized consideration, and, of course, due process is applicable at the trial phase and at the sentencing phase in capital and non짯capital trials.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: So it seems a little bit counterintuitive to me, the idea that, you know, the guilt phase in a very minor crime would have the exact same standard applicable to it as the guilt phase of a capital crime and then as the sentencing phase of a capital crime.

turns.text: If I may answer really briefly? Your Honor, we think the reason that's the case is that joint trials often enhance accuracy and fairness. So we agree that accuracy and fairness considerations are at their paramount in capital cases, but we do not think that militates for a different standard in capital cases because we think that the standard that the courts are applying is one that's going to generally enhance those values.

turns.name: MR. LIU
turns.text: Well, this Court, Your Honor, recognized in Satterwhite precisely how difficult it would be to conduct a harmless error analysis in a penalty phase, and that's precisely because there are so many factors involved. And so it is quite difficult for this Court, on a cold record, to go through the harmless error analysis.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: I 짯짯 I 짯짯

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA

So what 짯짯 what do you think are the 짯짯 are 짯짯 are the factors that would suggest the jury would have come out a different way had the rule that you urge been adopted? What 짯짯 what specifically? One is the shackling of the 짯짯 of the co짯defendant?

turns.name: MR. LIU
turns.text: Well, we are not representing Reginald Carr. We're not basing our claim around the shackling. Our 짯짯 our claim revolves around the evidence that Jonathan presented that Reginald had a corrupting influence on him while they were growing up. And that evidence, as the Kansas Supreme Court itself held at Petition Appendix 411, falls beyond the rubric of any valid sentencing factor.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: But, Mr. Liu, I mean, given the kind of evidence that was presented in this case, the idea that somebody was a lousy big brother seems pretty small on 짯짯 in the 짯짯 in the scale of things.

turns.name: MR. LIU
turns.text: Well, a few responses to that. To begin with, I think Your 짯짯 Your Honor is understating the evidence here. This wasn't just that Reginald was a lousy big brother. It was that he did things to Jonathan that turned Jonathan into the person who was capable of committing and even leading these crimes. So this wasn't just "lousy big brother" evidence, this was evidence that Reginald himself was the source of what caused Jonathan to do the things he did. But, Your Honor, I think it's also important to keep in mind two separate parts of the inquiry; there is the violation and the harmless error analysis. And this Court said in Stringer that even a thumb on the scales is enough to skew the weighing process in violation of the Eighth Amendment.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: But what 짯짯 what evidence that shouldn't have been admitted wouldn't have been admitted in the 짯짯 in the separate trial?

turns.name: MR. LIU
turns.text: Well, all this evidence, Mr. Chief Justice, that says Reginald was a corrupting influence on his brother, that wouldn't have come in in a separate trial for two reasons: Number one, as the Kansas Supreme Court held at Petition Appendix 411, that evidence was improper, nonstatutory, aggravating evidence, which is to say, all the aggravating factors before the prosecution pursuit in this case all had to do with the circumstances of the crime. But this evidence had nothing to do with the circumstances of what happened on December 15th, it had to do with the defendant's character concerning circumstances that occurred years, even decades, before the crimes at issue.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: Now, wait a minute. You're 짯짯 you're representing the brother who was the alleged corrupting influence, right?

turns.name: MR. LIU
turns.text: That's right, the corruptor.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: The corruptor was sentenced, by the same jury, to death. So how could it possibly be that without the corrupting action of your client, the jury would have sentenced your client to life even though the corruptee was sentenced to death? That doesn't seem to me at all likely.

turns.name: MR. LIU
turns.text: Well, to begin 짯짯 begin with, I don't think Jonathan Carr's sentence is a proper baseline to use. As my friend will argue in a few minute, his own sentence was prejudiced by severance. But even moving beyond that, there is every reason to believe that the jury viewed Jonathan as overall more culpable than Reginald. After all, the State never established who the shooter was here. If the jury believed that Jonathan was the shooter, the jury could have believed that Jonathan was so culpable that even with his evidence that he had been corrupted by Reginald, it would have given a life sentence. I think 짯짯

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: Let me 짯짯 let me put the crime to you. You tell me which of these descriptions of the 짯짯 of the crime are 짯짯 are incorrect. These two men broke into a house in which there were three men and two women. They ordered the five to remove their clothes, forced them into a closet. Over the course of three hours, they demanded that the two women perform various sexual acts on one another. They demanded at gunpoint that each of the three men have sexual intercourse with both women. Then Reginald drove the victims one by one to various ATMs to withdraw cash. Jonathan raped or attempted to rape both women twice, and Reginald raped Holly G., who later testified, once. Placing the three men, still naked, in the trunk of one of their cars, the cars drove all five to a soccer field, forced them to kneel in the snow, and shot them execution짯style in the back of the head. One of them, fortuitously, was not killed because 짯짯 I think it was a hair clip that she was wearing deflected the bullet. And she is the one who testified to all of this activity. And you truly think 짯짯 oh, and they ran over her too. After shooting her in the head, the car ran over her. You truly think that this jury, but for the fact that your client was a corruptor, would not have imposed the death penalty?

turns.name: MR. LIU
turns.text: We do, Justice Scalia. In your own opinion in last term in Glossip, you noted that the egregiousness of an offense is just one factor in determining whether a sentence is appropriate at the penalty phase. And Reginald Carr submitted a week and a half full of mitigation evidence that extenuated this offense. So I don't 짯짯 I think when you 짯짯 when you take all that all into account and consider the fact that this wasn't an easy case for the jury 짯짯 the jury, after all, deliberated a full day on what the 짯짯 what Kansas thinks is quite an easy case. I think the 짯짯 the scales of the weighing process were much more evenly balanced than that.

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: Do you think that the desire to spare Holly from having to testify more than once is a relevant factor here? And if so, how could that be 짯짯 how do you 짯짯 how would you propose to accommodate them?

turns.name: MR. LIU
turns.text: It's not a relevant factor and that's not my saying it, it's this Court saying it. This Court in Zafiro and Bruton has said that the benefits of joinder, while they may exist, cannot come at the price of constitutional rights. So while I 짯짯 I completely appreciate that severance would require some duplication of resources, that's precisely the sort of benefit that this Court has said cannot trump the Eighth Amendment.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: So if you're right, joinder being among the most common kind of thing that gangs and drugs and so forth, why won't the same argument be made over and over preventing 짯짯 requiring severance in dozens and dozens, perhaps hundreds of cases where the government tries people together? Because they will say there are different relationships among members of the gang. Those relationships 짯짯 some evidence would come in that would negative the relationship that would tend to show that this particular individual wasn't actually involved in this aspect of it, et cetera. That's why my experience on the Courts, lower courts particularly, leads me to think it's a very rarely accepted argument, severance.

turns.name: MR. LIU
turns.text: Well, you're right, Justice Breyer 짯짯

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: And that's why I'm interested in 짯짯 I mean, because if it were, you'd find it very unusual to try people together, which, in fact, is very usual. And so when you told me there is no separate thing for the death cases, then I imagine it would affect every criminal trial of gangs throughout the country.

turns.name: MR. LIU
turns.text: Well, I think 짯짯

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: And that is something that's concerning me and I am looking for an answer.

turns.name: MR. LIU
turns.text: You're absolutely right, Justice Breyer, that under our rule, the circumstances of each case are relevant. But I think what's also important is 짯짯 is that some 짯짯 the circumstances of some cases might not get rise for severance.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: That's what I'm looking for. Why is it you believe that if I were to decide in your favor on this case and find the sufficient prejudice to warrant severance here, I wouldn't at the same time be throwing a huge monkey wrench 짯짯

turns.name: MR. LIU
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: 짯짯 into the ordinary cases of gangs, drugs, et cetera, where joinder is very common and reversal is very rare.

turns.name: MR. LIU
turns.text: Because the evidence here is special in the sense that it fell beyond any existing sentencing factor.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: Are you proposing just separate sentencing hearings, or are you proposing separate juries for each of the brothers?

turns.name: MR. LIU
turns.text: Well, the Kansas Supreme Court in this case ordered as its remedy a new trial with two juries, but I don't think that is necessarily going to be the required remedy in every case. There are different ways, Justice Ginsburg, to solve the problems of sentencing.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: Let's take this case. If you had only one jury and Jonathan goes first, that jury, when it gets to Reginald's case, is not going to forget everything it heard in Jonathan's case.

turns.name: MR. LIU
turns.text: You're absolutely right, Justice Ginsburg. I don't think a sequential solution with Jonathan going first is going to work in this case precisely because the jury won't 짯짯 you won't be able to unring the bell of the 짯짯 precisely the evidence we think is prejudicial in this case. But that's not to say that a sequential solution isn't going to work in the mine run of cases. This case is special just because there 짯짯 there is some allegation that the prejudice ran both ways.

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: But the minute 짯짯 but the minute you defend the idea of two different juries, then you sacrifice the desirability and the possibility of consistency.

turns.name: MR. LIU
turns.text: Well, Justice Kennedy, there are many ways that States have developed to resolve the problem of inconsistent verdicts among different juries. One way is what the Georgia State has done, which is had proportionality review. They ask the appellate courts to look across many sentences to see if they are inconsistent. Another way the Federal government has a solution to this, is that let one jury know how the other jury sentenced the other defendant. That's 18 U.S.C. 3592(a)(4). And so there are ways to solve the problem of inconsistent verdicts across juries. But what's 짯짯

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: That sounds 짯짯 that sounds pretty prejudicial to me. If the second case, you say, oh, the jury considered this and individual circumstances, you know, these people act together, and by the way, a jury of your peers unanimously found beyond a reasonable doubt that this guy should be sentenced to death; now do whatever you want with this guy. That sounds pretty prejudicial to me.

turns.name: MR. LIU
turns.text: Well, it's 짯짯 it's 짯짯 if you think that is prejudicial, Mr. Chief Justice, then this proceeding was quite prejudicial too because the jury was asked to make that same exact comparison. But the specific prejudice we are alleging here is 짯짯 is unique to these or at least quite 짯짯 quite restricted to the circumstances of this case.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: Mr. Liu, could you, for me, just tell me what is the specific prejudice you're alleging here? In other words, tell me more than just, oh, he was a corrupting influence. What evidence particularly came in 짯짯

turns.name: MR. LIU
turns.text: Sure.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: 짯짯 and why do you think it might have mattered to the jury? Just give me your, like, best shot.

turns.name: MR. LIU
turns.text: Sure. Well, Kansas said there was no expert testimony on this, but that's wrong. Dr. Cunningham, at Joint Appendix pages 324 to 329, explained how Jonathan looked up to Reggie, and every time they would get together they would do drugs heavily together, that when 짯짯 that when they were ages 6 or 7, Reginald prompted someone to have sex with Jonathan. These are precisely the sort of evidence that shows that however bad you think Jonathan is, Reginald is worse because Reginald is the one who created the person Jonathan is. It's in a sense making Reginald doubly culpable for these offenses; that, well, whatever you think Jonathan did 짯짯 and yes, as Justice Scalia read, there were some horrific things done 짯짯 well, Reginald should be punished for not only everything Reginald did, but also everything Jonathan did. So this is precisely the sort of evidence that basically transfers all of Jonathan's actions to Reginald. And that's why it's so prejudicial when you get in the jury room, it's because it 짯짯 it skews the weighing process. And a skewing of the weighing process is an error this Court has recognized for 30 years. This isn't something that we 짯짯 we came up special for this case. This Court has recognized time and again that when a jury is told to consider an improper element in the weighing process, the weighing process is therefore skewed. And the only thing that can cure that error is either harmless error review or 짯짯

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: But it 짯짯

turns.name: MR. LIU
turns.text: 짯짯 reweighing by the State court.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: But it has to have been harmless inasmuch as the person who was influenced by your client also got the death penalty. How can you say that what made the difference was the fact that your client was a corrupting influence upon his younger brother?

turns.name: MR. LIU
turns.text: Well, Justice Scalia, the State never established the identity of the shooter, and that is the key horrific act in this case. If the jury believed Jonathan was the shooter, then the jury might have given him the death penalty anyway regardless of what Reginald did to him.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: I doubt whether that is the key. You really think that that's the only thing the jury is going to be focused on, is who pulled the trigger? My Lord.

turns.name: MR. LIU
turns.text: I don't think it's the only 짯짯 I don't think it's the only thing, but it's certainly the main thing given that we are here on a capital murder charge.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Do you think that on a retrial they cannot use the sister to prove that Reginald claimed he was the shooter?

turns.name: MR. LIU
turns.text: No. And I think that just highlights exactly how narrow our rule is. That the evidence that Temica said that Reginald told her that Reginald was the shooter goes to the circumstances of the crime. And that sort of finger pointing, who shot who on the day of the crime, is going to fall comfortably within an existing sentencing factor, and as a result, is not going to skew the weighing process.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: So the only issue you think was prejudicial was that he was a corruptor?

turns.name: MR. LIU
turns.text: Absolutely.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: The evidence.

turns.name: MR. LIU
turns.text: And for reasons I 짯짯 I gave to Justice Kagan, that evidence was extremely prejudicial. It skewed the weighing process because it transferred Jonathan's culpability back onto Reginald's shoulders. If I may, I'd just like to address a few points raised by my friend from Kansas. He said the instructions solve the problem. Well, none of the instructions told the jury to consider this evidence about the corrupting influence only as to Jonathan. Now, on the contrary, the instructions 짯짯 Instruction No. 2 told the jury consider the evidence applicable to each defendant. Well, this evidence was equally applicable to Reginald as it was to Jonathan. After all, it was Reginald 짯짯 Reginald's actions that were at issue. My friends also point to the instruction on statutory aggravators. But that disregards an entire swath of evidence that the jury heard that it could consider against Reginald. This was evidence, anti짯mitigation evidence that the jury considered, could have caused them to remove weight from the mitigation side of the scale. And the instructions left the jury completely free to consider this evidence in anti짯mitigation. My friends also suggest that I'm here only arguing an error of State law. Well, no less than the Petitioners were in Zant, Stringer, and 짯짯 and Sanders. Each of those cases recognizes that a skewing of the weighing process has to be based on State law premises. That's why this Court in Zant went so far as to certify a question to the Georgia Supreme Court. Yes, it's true that to understand the effect on the weighing process, you have to look at the State law. But the mere admission of evidence isn't what causes the violation here. It's the admission of the evidence, followed by the fact the jury was required to consider it. And it was required to consider it because it was part and parcel of Jonathan's mitigating evidence. And this Court has said time and again that a defendant has a constitutional right for the jury, not only to consider but to listen to a defendant's mitigating evidence. So by considering this evidence for Jonathan, the jury necessarily considered it against Reginald. After all, if you're told that Reginald had a corrupting influence on Jonathan, you can't separate that as something for Jonathan and something for Reginald. It's equally applicable to both. My friend also suggested that we 짯짯 we rejected a two짯jury solution before trial. Well, we rejected that, not because of it wouldn't work to solve any prejudice in the penalty phase, but because it wouldn't work to solve any prejudice in the guilt phase. And remember, the Kansas Supreme Court found that it was actually error to keep the guilt phase joined, so we were perfectly appropriate in objecting to a two짯jury solution at that phase of the trial.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: Can you explain that again? I thought you 짯짯 you sought only a severance at the sentencing phase.

turns.name: MR. LIU
turns.text: No. That's not 짯짯 that's not 짯짯

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: You sought severance totally?

turns.name: MR. LIU
turns.text: Exactly. Prior to trial, on pages 25 and 26 of the Joint Appendix, we moved for severance of the entire thing. And if you look at those pages, you'll see Jonathan's attorney, Jonathan's own attorney, saying that if the penalty phase is joined, he is going to have to present evidence that, quote, "there's no way the State would be able to introduce if Reginald was sitting here alone." Well, his attorney was exactly right. That's exactly what happened many months later. I see that my time is up.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Mr. Green.

turns.name: MR. GREEN
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: I'd like to first try and change the narrative here with some illustrations about the difference between the mitigation cases of both defendants. That's easily seen in the testimony of the experts. Reginald Carr presented evidence from a neuroscientist by the name of Dr. Woltersdorf that said his client had been diagnosed a 짯짯 an incurable sociopath. Jonathan Carr's forensic psychologist, clinical and forensic psychologist, got up and testified that 짯짯 that Jonathan was sensitive, he was affectionate, he had attempted suicide, and what he really was, contrary to what Mr. McAllister represented to you, was 짯짯 was depressed and schizophrenic. That is 짯짯 that is a unique illustration of exactly how different these two defendants were in their presentation, and why, again, a sequential penalty phase, even if they had been separate, wouldn't work. Reginald says he didn't want to hear or he thought it would be prejudicial if the jury heard evidence from the 짯짯 from the 짯짯 from Jonathan's witnesses about Reginald being a 짯짯 a corrupting influence. On the other side, Jonathan doesn't want the jury to hear from Reginald's expert. And to answer your question, Justice Kagan, that's the evidence that would not have been introduced. Justice Woltersdorf's 짯짯 or excuse me 짯짯 Dr. Woltersdorf's testimony about incurable sociopathy would not have been relevant or admissible 짯짯

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: Maybe I'm missing something, but if 짯짯 if your client's strategy was to make Reginald as bad 짯짯 the bad actor in the case, why didn't that evidence actually go hand in hand with your client's strategy, which was to say it's all on Reginald?

turns.name: MR. GREEN
turns.text: One 짯짯 one would think that it would in 짯짯 in a typical case, and it may be that some jurors had inferred that way. The problem was that 짯짯 that the fact that the two brothers were sitting together allowed the prosecutors to repeatedly paint them with the same brush. And 짯짯 and if we take a look, for example, at JA 402, the prosecutor says to the jury in her opening argument at the beginning of the penalty 짯짯 or at the conclusion of the penalty phase, ladies and gentlemen, they have the same eye color. They are now wearing glasses, although their mother said Reginald doesn't need them, they share some DNA.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: So this 짯짯 maybe you can, in your experience, cure what's bothering me about the case. It's nothing to do with the facts, or for this 짯짯 this concern. It has to do with 짯짯 with Zafiro.

turns.name: MR. GREEN
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: And that's the ordinary case of joint trials, right? And what the Court says is you can have the joinder as long as there isn't a serious risk that the joint trial would compromise 짯짯 it doesn't say harmless error, it says compromise 짯짯 a specific trial right, or prevent the jury from making a reliable judgment. That doesn't talk about harmless error. Now, that's the standard that's used in dozens and dozens and dozens of cases. Or if, in this case, we start talking about harmless error, and that you can't have the joint proceeding if there is error that is not harmless. Well, what have we done to that sentence? And what have we done to the trials that are joint in hundreds of cases that don't involve murder or death? That is the legal problem that is worrying me. And you will either cure that worry or say I've made some elementary mistake. It has nothing to do with your case. Say what you want, but I want to hear what you think about it.

turns.name: MR. GREEN
turns.text: I don't 짯짯 I don't think you've made any mistake at all, Justice Breyer. Indeed, I think that 짯짯 that the State of Kansas and the Solicitor General are offering nothing more than 짯짯 than the Zafiro test and using slightly different language. On page 15 of our 짯짯

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: So we should not use the words "harmless error." We should quote from Zafiro and say could they make a reliable judgment? Now, that sounds harder on you, because if all you have to show is the error was harmful, it sounds like an easier task you have than to show that the juror 짯짯 jury wasn't reliable. Or are they the same thing in your opinion?

turns.name: MR. GREEN
turns.text: Well, let me 짯짯 I think they're the same thing and I think that's a generalization, but we've 짯짯 we've offered a test that actually specifies it a little bit more, Justice Breyer. In terms of saying that 짯짯 that the test should be whether there is a reasonable risk 짯짯 and I will explain the difference between reasonable and serious in a minute 짯짯 but whether there was a reasonable risk that there would be evidence introduced, material, prejudicial evidence introduced that would 짯짯 that would, in fact, not have been introduced in a severed penalty짯phase proceeding. And to go back to Justice Kagan's question about the intuitive difference between these two things, they're between 짯짯 excuse me 짯짯 trials and 짯짯 and penalty짯phase proceedings, the answer is that's because in a 짯짯 in a trial, the 짯짯 the relative culpability of the defendants makes a big difference with respect to who's a conspirator, who's not a conspirator, who's a principal, who's an aider and abettor. But when we turn to the penalty phase, the inquiry changes, as Mr. Liu indicated 짯짯 it changes from exactly what happened to who this person is and whether or not the jury wants to put this person to death.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Who 짯짯 who 짯짯 if you have separate proceedings, who 짯짯 who gets to go second? Because obviously that person will have a significant advantage since they'll see all of the evidence presented in the 짯짯 in the other proceeding.

turns.name: MR. GREEN
turns.text: Well, in 짯짯

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: And most of the evidence here was overlapping, so that at least you'll have 짯짯 you'll be able to see what the State thought about that evidence. You have sort of a dry run if you're the second person.

turns.name: MR. GREEN
turns.text: Well, in this case, respectfully, no, Mr. Chief Justice, because what the 짯짯 what the State basically said was we're relying on our evidence from the 짯짯 from the trial phase 짯짯 or from the guilt phase of this capital trial. That's exactly what the prosecutor told the jurors when they turned in to the penalty phase, and all that the prosecution did in the penalty phase was cross짯examine the defense witnesses.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Well, but that cross짯examination is important. Were there any common defense witnesses?

turns.name: MR. GREEN
turns.text: There were family members. So 짯짯 so it is true 짯짯

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: So at least it would be 짯짯 it would be helpful for the second 짯짯 the person who goes second to know, well, what did the prosecutor talk 짯짯 how did the prosecutor attempt to cross짯examine that witness, because I am going to call the same witness, and her testimony is going to be a lot better the second time around because you will know exactly what the cross짯examination questions are going to be.

turns.name: MR. GREEN
turns.text: That might happen. I 짯짯 it 짯짯 it could well be that that would be 짯짯 I would consider that a 짯짯 a minor advantage, Your Honor. But that could be handled by the judge in 짯짯 with protective orders, with orders about 짯짯 rules against witnesses and 짯짯 and folks in the courtroom who will have another proceeding. But I would submit 짯짯 and then with respect to witnesses who might have been traumatized by these events, that 짯짯 the 짯짯 you know, a trial court could easily have a simultaneous penalty proceedings so that that witness would only have to testify once. The defense team on one side wouldn't get an advantage over the other. There would be two juries in two different courtrooms, same week, same days, the 짯짯 the witness can go back and forth between courtrooms, and there is none of the advantage 짯짯

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: You would have 짯짯 the State would have to put on, basically, its guilt phase case again at the penalty phase, right?

turns.name: MR. GREEN
turns.text: Yes, it would have to do that, but I would imagine that 짯짯 that with respect to cases like this, the defense attorneys are going to move right into what counts as stipulations; we will read testimony 짯짯

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: Well, I am sure you'd like to stipulate to all the facts that were proven at the guilt phase, but I doubt that the State is going to want to stipulate to those.

turns.name: MR. GREEN
turns.text: And the State might do that. And again, I think a trial judge could easily handle that by expediting matters. And this is what happens in a lot of the Federal trials. I would refer the Court to the 짯짯 to the brief of the Promise of Justice Initiative which shows that when we have joint trials, we get joint results. When we have severed trials, we get severed results. 25 for 25, joint trials, same result for the defendants. Woodson says we must have individualized sentencing at the penalty phase, so I do not see why the Zafiro test would not 짯짯 would 짯짯 would work for penalty phase proceedings without some sort of change. There has to be some recognition that 짯짯 that penalty phase proceedings are different than 짯짯 than all the other armed robbery, drug cases.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: So how is that different from what the government said?

turns.name: MR. GREEN
turns.text: I 짯짯 so I think we ought to lower the risk standard. We ought to lower the risk standard to a reasonable risk standard, not 짯짯 and that's what we propose in our brief at page 15. We should lower the standard because that would recognize the acute need for reliability and accuracy when it comes to penalty phase proceedings and the decision whether somebody is going to live or die.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: You said you were going to make a distinction between serious and reasonable earlier.

turns.name: MR. GREEN
turns.text: Right. So a serious risk may be treated as a 짯짯 as a preponderance or a 짯짯 or above. With respect to reasonable, we're at a 짯짯 we're at a 짯짯 a 짯짯 maybe a likelihood. Maybe that's 짯짯 maybe that's perceived as 30 percent versus 55 percent or 60 percent, something like that. And indeed, this Court said in Boyde, with respect to reasonable likelihood, that is not a preponderance standard. If the Court has no further questions, we would urge 짯짯

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Mr. McAllister, five minutes. MR. McALLISTER: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: I will try to be brief. The only Eighth Amendment implication that this Court has recognized that really applies to this case is I go back to the Romano case where the Court said the admission of evidence that might or might not be in violation of State law is not an Eighth Amendment concern. The only time the Eighth 짯짯 Eighth Amendment comes into play is when the evidence is 짯짯 involves constitutionally protected conduct. An example would be Dawson v. Delaware, where they sought a capital sentence. He was a member of the Aryan Brotherhood in prison. It had nothing to do with an aggregating factor. That was First Amendment짯protected activity. The Court said that kind of thing could be an Eighth Amendment problem if you use it against the defendant. Their cases 짯짯 I could be wrong, but I think all of their cases, Zant, Stringer, the others that they refer to skewing the weighing process, are not about admission of evidence, they are about invalid aggravating factors. So you've basically told the jury there is something else, literally, on the scale, an aggravating factor that later the State says, no, that was 짯짯 that was not correct, that should not have been there. That's distinguishable from this case. I 짯짯 I agree completely with Justice Scalia's point, and the dissenting justice made it in the Kansas Supreme Court: If the evidence 짯짯 the corrupting influence evidence was so prejudicial to Reginald, why did Jonathan also get a death sentence? And I would close by saying that we all agree, I think, that the Constitution values accuracy and fairness. But it also values finality. And each of these individuals received an individualized sentence, presented all the evidence they wanted to. The jury was instructed to consider them individually. And if we were to undo this now, it would be very difficult for Kansas to go back. We'd be talking about having to redo all of the guilt phase evidence. And, again, you get into questions, separate proceedings, traumatizing victims yet again. None of that is necessary, because at the end of the day they got a fundamentally fair proceeding that gave each of them the sentence they deserved and the jury found warranted, both under the facts of this case and the law of Kansas. We would ask that you reverse the Kansas Supreme Court on this point.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Mr. McAllister, I am sorry, but even if we do this and say that the sentencings didn't need to be severed, didn't the Kansas court hold on another ground that they were entitled to a new trial? MR. McALLISTER: Yes. So that's the third question presented in the Petition, which my understanding is that is something the Court could reconsider. It's not denied. The Court granted Questions 1 and 3. The other question that we presented was the only other ground that the Kansas Supreme Court gave for reversal, that's a Confrontation Clause violation in terms of some of the hearsay evidence presented in the sentencing proceedings. So if the Court were to reverse on these, deny on that question, then the Kansas Supreme Court could, in fact, rely on that ground. But our hope would be that the Court would do something different than that if you reverse on these two questions. Unless there are further questions, thank you.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. The cases are submitted.
